item.title: Man accused of threatening to burn Suva released as investigation continues
item.description: Police say the interview process has been suspended, while investigators await the results of a digital forensic examination of the suspect's mobile phone,. Police are reminding people to use social media responsibly, saying freedom of speech and expression come with responsibility.
item.content:encoded:
Police say the interview process has been suspended, while investigators await the results of a digital forensic examination of the suspect's mobile phone,.

Police are reminding people to use social media responsibly, saying freedom of speech and expression come with responsibility.

They warn that anyone who makes or shares posts encouraging violence or threatening public safety may be investigated for offences including urging political violence, criminal intimidation and incitement to violence.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Police should swiftly arrest those making threats against Fiji's peace - Turaga
item.description: Rashika Kumar , Antornette Vuniuca · 20/07/2026 Acting Attorney General Siromi Turaga says if there are people making inflammatory comments against the peace of this country, then the police should be swift and take the person into police custody.
item.content:encoded:
Rashika Kumar , Antornette Vuniuca · 20/07/2026

Acting Attorney General Siromi Turaga says if there are people making inflammatory comments against the peace of this country, then the police should be swift and take the person into police custody.

This comes as the police have arrested a man for allegedly issuing a threat on social media to remove Fiji's Constitution or he will burn down the nation's capital.

Turaga says nobody is supposed to be making those threats and the government is steadfast.

He says there are no issues with the constitutional review process, which remains on schedule for a 31st August submission to the President.

Turaga stresses that if people have any concerns, he assures them that this government came with the promise to deliver a new Constitution, and they are doing that.

He also stresses that any unnecessary threats are irresponsible.

The Minister for Justice is urging people of Fiji to refrain from making inflammatory comments or threats that undermine peace and security.

He adds that we have enjoyed a wonderful environment of collaboration between the government and the security forces, and will continue to maintain that.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Police launch dedicated Scams Section after $1.3m lost to fraud since 2017
item.description: More than $1.3 million has been lost through 1,672 reported scam cases across Fiji since 2017, prompting the Fiji Police Force to establish a dedicated Scams Section to strengthen investigations into cyber-enabled crime and fraud. Speaking at the launch of the Annual National Scams Awareness Week at the Grand Pacific Hotel in Suva this morning, Commissioner of Police Rusiate Tudravu says the speci
item.content:encoded:
More than $1.3 million has been lost through 1,672 reported scam cases across Fiji since 2017, prompting the Fiji Police Force to establish a dedicated Scams Section to strengthen investigations into cyber-enabled crime and fraud.

Speaking at the launch of the Annual National Scams Awareness Week at the Grand Pacific Hotel in Suva this morning, Commissioner of Police Rusiate Tudravu says the specialised unit has been created to deal with all scam-related reports as authorities anticipate a rise in scam activity.

Tudravu says the new section comprises specialised investigators equipped with the digital literacy and forensic expertise needed to track cyber-enabled syndicates.

He warns that while police and financial institutions continue to strengthen their security systems, scammers often succeed because people unknowingly give away sensitive information.

He says scammers exploit human psychology by preying on hope, urgency and vulnerability rather than weaknesses in the law.

Police statistics show that the 1,672 scam reports were lodged at 21 police stations nationwide, with the Western Division recording the highest number of cases, followed by the Southern, Central, Eastern and Northern divisions.

Cases still under investigation in the Western Division alone involve more than $500,000 .

Of the total reports received, 338 have progressed to the prosecution stage, while 11 cases have been finalised, including two acquittals after victims failed to appear in court despite being summoned.

Tudravu is urging members of the public to remain vigilant and never share one-time passwords or other sensitive banking information, saying Fiji's strongest defence against scams is a digitally resilient population.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj
